diagnostic_signals.explanation: CGWB defines groundwater stress classes based on extraction as a percentage of net annual recharge. In semi-consolidated aquifers of the Malwa plateau and Bundelkhand, storativity is moderate but spatially heterogeneous — block-level SOGE values above 70% indicate that extraction is approaching or exceeding sustainable recharge limits. The Central Highlands (AER-10) have documented increasing extraction pressure driven by rabi wheat and soybean irrigation, making SOGE a primary diagnostic indicator.

condition.qualitative_description: The linear trend in annual groundwater recharge (P - ET - Runoff) is negative and the multi-year mean is also negative, indicating chronic depletion rather than a single drought year anomaly.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: A sustained negative trend in delta_g combined with a negative multi-year mean confirms that outflows (ET + runoff) persistently exceed precipitation inputs, leaving no surplus for aquifer recharge. In the sub-humid Central Highlands, rainfall of 900–1200 mm is concentrated in the kharif season; if ET and runoff consume this surplus, rabi and zaid seasons face chronic groundwater deficits. This pattern is consistent with global evidence of accelerating groundwater-level declines in cropland-intensive dry regions.

condition.qualitative_description: Aquifer is classified as sedimentary soft rock (consistent with Vindhyan sandstone and Deccan trap margins in Bundelkhand/Malwa) with less than 30% alluvial lithology, indicating limited recharge capacity and moderate-to-low storativity.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Semi-consolidated sedimentary aquifers (Vindhyan sandstone, weathered Deccan basalt margins) in AER-10 have lower storativity than deep alluvial systems. Where alluvial cover is limited (<30%), natural recharge pathways are restricted to fracture zones and thin weathered horizons. This geological vulnerability amplifies extraction-driven depletion: even moderate over-extraction rapidly lowers water tables. The contrast with alluvial aquifers of the Indo-Gangetic Plain — where storage buffers seasonal deficits — is critical for management design.

condition.qualitative_description: Fewer than 10 cumulative SWC works in the micro-watershed over the MGNREGA record, indicating low investment in recharge infrastructure relative to extraction pressure.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Low MGNREGA-funded SWC investment means that natural recharge opportunities — check dams, percolation ponds, trench bunds on slopes — have not been exploited. In plateau and slope terrain of Bundelkhand and Malwa, runoff from sloping catchments is rapid and high-volume; without interception structures, monsoon recharge potential is lost. India has a long history of tank and pond-based recharge, and government investment in percolation structures has been shown to substantially augment aquifer recharge in drought-prone areas. Low SWC counts signal both an amplifier of depletion and an intervention opportunity.

condition.qualitative_description: Farmers report that they have had to deepen borewells or dug-wells over the past 5–10 years, or that shallow wells that previously yielded water year-round now run dry in the rabi or zaid season. This is a direct field indicator of declining static water levels in semi-consolidated aquifers.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Well deepening is a reliable proxy for groundwater table decline. In semi-consolidated aquifers of the Central Highlands, productive zones are typically within 20–50m depth; progressive deepening beyond this range signals that the saturated zone is retreating. Seasonal drying of shallow wells during rabi (November–March) is a particularly diagnostic symptom in this context, where groundwater is critical for wheat and gram irrigation. This variable is not available in the framework data and must be elicited from farmers.

overall_reasoning_note: Groundwater stress in sub-humid semi-consolidated aquifers of AER-10 (Bundelkhand, Malwa) should be confirmed by at least two primary signals: elevated SOGE classification, chronic negative delta_g trend, or farmer-reported well deepening. The geological context (sig_03) amplifies vulnerability but does not confirm stress independently. Low SWC investment (sig_04) is an amplifier and intervention signal. A key diagnostic distinction from purely meteorological drought is that SOGE remains elevated even in good rainfall years — if soge_dev_percent exceeds 70% during a year with near-normal precipitation, extraction rather than recharge failure is the primary driver. Block-level SOGE may understate local stress in heterogeneous plateau terrain; micro-watershed-level delta_g trends are more spatially precise. Always elicit borewell density and well depth trends from farmers before finalising diagnosis.

confounders.confounder: Meteorological drought reducing recharge and causing apparent groundwater stress without chronic over-extraction
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Compare trend_annual_delta_g_mm against annual_precipitation_mm time series. If negative delta_g years coincide exclusively with drought years (low precipitation) and soge_dev_percent remains below 70%, drought-driven recharge failure rather than over-extraction is the primary driver. If SOGE is elevated (>70%) even in normal rainfall years, extraction is the dominant cause.

confounders.confounder: High runoff from sloping plateau terrain reducing in-field moisture and causing crop water stress without groundwater depletion per se
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Check terrain_cluster_id and mean_annual_runoff_mm. If runoff is high (>200mm/year) and soge_dev_percent is low (<50%), the primary stress may be surface water loss rather than groundwater depletion. In this case, in-situ moisture conservation (bunding, mulching) rather than groundwater recharge is the priority intervention.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Over the past 5–10 years, have farmers in this area had to deepen their borewells or dug-wells to find water? If yes, by roughly how many feet or metres have they had to go deeper? Do shallow wells that used to give water throughout the year now run dry in winter (rabi) or summer (zaid)?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: If farmers confirm progressive well deepening of more than 3–5 metres over 5–8 years, or report seasonal drying of previously perennial wells, this strongly confirms the groundwater stress pathway in semi-consolidated aquifers. Rapid deepening (>1m/year) indicates imminent well failure and elevates severity to critical.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Approximately how many borewells or tubewells are there in your village or the surrounding 2–3 km area? Would you say there are very few (less than 10), a moderate number (10–50), or a large number (more than 50)? Have many new borewells been drilled in the last 5 years?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: High borewell density (>50 per village) in a semi-consolidated aquifer with moderate SOGE confirms localised over-extraction not captured by block-level statistics. Rapid recent drilling activity suggests accelerating extraction pressure and increases diagnostic confidence for the groundwater stress pathway.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Have farmers noticed that borewell or well water has become saltier, leaves white or yellowish deposits on fields or pipes, or causes crop damage that was not observed previously? Is the water from deeper borewells different in taste or smell from shallower wells?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: Salinity in semi-consolidated aquifers can indicate penetration into deeper saline horizons as shallow freshwater zones are depleted — a secondary consequence of over-extraction. If confirmed, it changes solution priorities toward demand management and aquifer zoning rather than simple recharge augmentation.

sources.title: National Compilation of Dynamic Ground Water Resources of India 2022
sources.relevance_note: Defines SOGE class thresholds (Safe <70%, Semi-critical 70–90%, Critical 90–100%, Over-exploited >100%) used in sig_01; provides block-level groundwater development data for Madhya Pradesh and Uttar Pradesh including Bundelkhand and Malwa regions.

sources.title: Addressing Depletion in Alluvial Aquifers
sources.relevance_note: Argues that management approaches must account for geological differences between aquifer types; documents that hard-rock and semi-consolidated aquifer management requires different institutional and technical solutions than alluvial systems, directly relevant to AER-10 context.

sources.title: Rapid groundwater decline and some cases of recovery in aquifers globally
sources.relevance_note: Documents that rapid groundwater-level declines (>0.5m/year) are widespread in dry regions with extensive croplands, and that declines have accelerated in 30% of regional aquifers globally; supports the use of trend-based signals for diagnosis and notes that policy changes and managed aquifer recharge can reverse depletion.

sources.title: Sixty years of global progress in managed aquifer recharge
sources.relevance_note: Documents India's extensive history of tank, pond, and percolation structure-based aquifer recharge; notes that government and NGO investment in percolation tanks to infiltrate monsoon runoff in drought-prone areas has been substantial, supporting the SWC-based intervention recommendations and the diagnostic value of low nrega_swc_count.

sources.title: Groundwater use for irrigation – a global inventory
sources.relevance_note: Establishes India as the country with the largest area equipped for groundwater irrigation globally (39 million ha), contextualising the extraction pressure in regions like Bundelkhand and Malwa where irrigation expansion has outpaced recharge capacity.
